blurb: Over 300 arcade and pinball machines, retro consoles, tabletop tournaments, and every kind of game you grew up with (and plenty you didn't). The Southern-Fried Gaming Expo is back at the Marriott Renaissance Waverly in late July, and it is one of those events that works for basically everyone in the family. Teens will disappear for hours. Adults who think they are just tagging along will end up in a Pac-Man bracket. Tickets go on sale in advance, so check the website and get ahead of it.
scoring_notes: Strong match for East Cobb's teen-heavy family demographic. The Waverly location is extremely close to East Cobb. Uniqueness score reflects that it is an annual event but one with genuine scale and a niche appeal that most readers won't have attended before. The nostalgia angle works well for the median age of 42. NOTE: Date is July 31 to August 2, which is outside the 'this week' window but was included as a future feature option.

blurb: The Georgia Renaissance Festival runs through May 31, which means you still have a couple of weekends to catch it before it closes for the year. Jousting, roasted turkey legs, costumed characters, and vendors selling everything from chainmail to hand-blown glass. It is a full day out, and the kind of place where teens who think they are too cool for family stuff end up having a genuinely good time. Fairburn is about 45 minutes from East Cobb, but it is worth the drive. Once it closes, you wait a whole year.
scoring_notes: The closing-weekend angle creates genuine urgency, which bumps the uniqueness score above what a recurring annual event would normally get. East Cobb's mix of teens and established families is a strong match for the Renaissance Festival format. The drive to Fairburn is a trade-off but not a dealbreaker for a $118K median income household. Good closer for the newsletter window.

blurb: If you like browsing art without the pressure of a gallery, this is your weekend. The Alpharetta Arts Streetfest at Wills Park brings together artisans, vendors, street performers, and kids activities across two full days. It is close, it is free, and the weather in late May is usually just right for wandering outside with no agenda. Grab the family or go solo. Either way, you will find something worth stopping for.
scoring_notes: Strong demographic fit for East Cobb's educated, higher-income households who appreciate arts and culture. The mix of adult browsing and kids activities works well for the newsletter's blend of established families and empty nesters. Loses a point on uniqueness since arts festivals recur, but May 23-24 timing is squarely in the newsletter window and the Alpharetta location is very accessible from East Cobb.

blurb: You don't see 46-foot dragon boats racing across a Georgia lake every weekend. The Acworth-Cobb Dragon Boat Festival brings ornately decorated boats, live drumming, and real racing energy to Lake Allatoona. It is free to watch, rooted in ancient Chinese tradition, and supports local cancer patients and survivors. Whether you have paddled before or have no idea what dragon boat racing even is, it is the kind of event you show up to once and end up staying for hours.
scoring_notes: High uniqueness score because dragon boat racing is genuinely unusual and visually striking. Good fit for East Cobb families and curious adults. The charitable angle (supporting cancer patients) adds emotional resonance. The Acworth location is a reasonable drive from East Cobb. NOTE: The source context places this 'this Saturday' in relation to early May coverage; the actual 2026 date could not be confirmed from the candidate data. Should be verified before publishing.
